This distinction matters because a payroll item that is misclassified can create filing errors, under-withholding, or future correction work.
Send to the team:
- “Please confirm taxability before coding any employee cost so payroll and reporting are correct from the start.”
Key point:
- Internal labels do not decide tax treatment.
Official links:
- IRS Tax Withholding
- IRS Publication 15-B
55. Q: What taxes may apply to payroll?
A: Payroll can trigger several tax obligations at once, and the exact combination depends on the worker status, the employment arrangement, and the applicable jurisdiction. In a common payroll setting, the employer may need to withhold income tax from employee pay, calculate employee social contributions, and record employer payroll taxes or statutory contributions separately. The organization may also have reporting duties for wage statements, remittances, and annual filings, all of which must agree to the payroll register and the general ledger. The key control point is that payroll tax treatment should be identified before the run is finalized, not after the bank file has been released. If a worker is misclassified or a deduction is missed, the error affects the employee, the employer, and the organization’s compliance record. Payroll tax handling therefore needs a clear calendar, clear responsibility, and a review step before each payment cycle.
Send to the team:
- “Please confirm all payroll tax types before each payroll run so withholding, employer taxes, and filing are complete.”
Key point:
- Payroll tax treatment depends on worker status and local law.
Official links:
- IRS Tax Withholding
- IRS Topic No. 751
- IRS Publication 15
56. Q: Why should payroll taxes be monitored carefully?
A: Payroll taxes should be monitored continuously because payroll is one of the most time-sensitive and penalty-sensitive parts of finance. A missed filing, a late remittance, or a wrong liability balance can quickly create interest charges, statutory penalties, and employee confidence issues. Careful monitoring also helps finance see whether liabilities being accrued in the books actually match what is owed to the tax authority and what has been paid from the bank. In practice, this means reconciling payroll registers, liability accounts, filings, and payment confirmations every cycle, not only at year-end. When the organization treats payroll taxes as a routine control area rather than an occasional compliance task, it is easier to avoid surprises, correct errors early, and maintain a clean payroll balance sheet. This is especially important in NGOs where payroll cost often forms a large share of program and support expenditure.
Send to the team:
- “Please reconcile payroll tax liabilities before each filing deadline and clear differences immediately.”
Key point:
- Payroll tax monitoring prevents expensive compliance and reporting errors.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- GAO Green Book
57. Q: Who is responsible for payroll tax compliance?
A: The employer is ultimately responsible for payroll tax compliance, even when day-to-day processing is handled by a payroll officer, finance team, HR team, or external provider. Outsourcing may shift the task, but it does not remove accountability from the organization. That means management must still ensure the payroll system is set up correctly, the right taxes are applied, filings happen on time, and payment evidence is retained. A proper responsibility structure should identify one named owner for the payroll tax calendar, one reviewer for calculations, and one approver for remittance. Clear ownership is especially important where multiple staff touch payroll data, because unclear responsibility often leads to missed deadlines or unexplained errors. The organization should be able to show who checked what, when they checked it, and what was done if an issue was found.
Send to the team:
- “Please assign one owner for payroll tax compliance and one reviewer for the calculation and filing cycle.”
Key point:
- The employer owns payroll tax compliance.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- IRS Tax Withholding
- GAO Green Book
58. Q: What is the risk of late payroll tax filing?
A: Late payroll tax filing can trigger a chain of operational and financial problems. At the most basic level, it can create penalties and interest, but it can also distort the liability balance in the ledger, cause follow-up work with the tax authority, and force finance to spend time on corrections rather than planning. In an NGO environment, late filing also weakens confidence among employees, donors, auditors, and regulators because payroll is expected to be both predictable and controlled. Late filing may indicate a broader weakness such as missing reminders, poor segregation of duties, weak review of payroll calendars, or lack of a backup person when the main payroll processor is absent. If the organization repeatedly misses payroll deadlines, the issue is no longer only compliance; it becomes a control design problem that should be documented and fixed. The best response is to use a filing calendar, confirmation checklist, and escalation rule for any delay.
Send to the team:
- “Please treat payroll tax deadlines as non-negotiable control points and escalate any expected delay immediately.”
Key point:
- Late filing is both a compliance failure and a control failure.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- GAO Green Book
59. Q: How should payroll tax liabilities be recorded?
A: Payroll tax liabilities should be recognized in the same period that the payroll is earned, not only when the cash payment is made to employees or tax authorities. That means the payroll journal should capture gross pay, employee deductions, employer tax costs, and the corresponding liabilities at month-end or pay-date, depending on the organization’s accounting basis and cutoff rules. Separate liability accounts should be used where practical so employee withholdings, employer tax obligations, pension-style deductions, and other statutory payables are not mixed together. This improves traceability and helps the accountant see what is still owed, what has already been remitted, and what is still sitting in cash. Recording the liability promptly also makes reconciliation much easier because the ledger already reflects the obligation before the payment file goes out. If the timing is inconsistent, the payroll balance can drift and create a mismatch between the ledger, tax return, and bank activity.
Send to the team:
- “Please book payroll liabilities in the same period as the payroll run and keep employee and employer amounts separate.”
Key point:
- Payroll liabilities should be accrued promptly and clearly separated.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- IRS Topic No. 751
- GAO Green Book
60. Q: How should tax payments be reconciled?
A: Tax payment reconciliation should connect four things: the payroll register, the liability accounts, the filed return or remittance schedule, and the bank payment evidence. Each of those sources should point to the same gross amount, the same due date, and the same period of payroll activity. If the liability balance does not match the filed amount, finance should check whether the issue is caused by timing, a payroll correction, a missed journal entry, or an actual underpayment. Reconciliation should not stop at “cash left the bank”; it should prove that the organization’s books, filings, and bank records agree. In a well-run process, the reconciler can explain any difference, document any timing gap, and mark the item as cleared only when the obligation is fully settled. This creates a defensible record for audit and for tax authority review.
Send to the team:
- “Please reconcile payroll tax payments to the ledger, filed returns, and bank confirmations every cycle.”
Key point:
- Reconciliation proves the tax was filed and paid correctly.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
61. Q: What is statutory deduction?
A: A statutory deduction is any amount the law requires the employer to withhold from an employee’s pay. Common examples include payroll taxes, social insurance contributions, and lawful garnishments or court-ordered deductions where they are recognized by the applicable legal framework. The important feature of a statutory deduction is that it is not optional once the legal condition applies and the relevant worker or payment type is inside scope. Because the deduction is required by law, the employer must calculate it correctly, record it accurately, and remit it on time. The payroll team should not rely on informal requests or employee preference when a deduction is statutory in nature. Instead, the deduction should flow from the legal rule, the payroll setup, and the approved payroll register.
Send to the team:
- “Please apply all legally required deductions before net pay is released and keep the legal basis on file.”
Key point:
- Statutory deductions are required by law.
Official links:
- IRS Tax Withholding
- IRS Publication 15
62. Q: What deductions may be taken from employee pay?
A: Employee deductions can include several different categories, and each category needs a clear legal or policy basis. Some deductions are statutory, such as taxes or legally required social contributions. Others may be voluntary or contractual, such as employee benefit contributions, loan repayments, salary advances, or approved savings deductions. A well-controlled payroll process should distinguish deductions that are mandatory, deductions that require employee consent, and deductions that exist only because an employment contract or policy specifically allows them. Each deduction should show the reason, amount, timing, and evidence for the deduction so the employee can understand it and finance can defend it later. This matters because the same payroll run can contain many deduction types, but only proper classification keeps the organization compliant and transparent.
Send to the team:
- “Please verify the legal and policy basis before any deduction is taken and keep supporting authorization on file.”
Key point:
- Every deduction needs a valid legal or policy basis.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- IRS Publication 15-B
- GAO Green Book
63. Q: How should employee deductions be authorized?
A: Employee deductions should be authorized in a way that matches the type of deduction being made. If the deduction is statutory, the legal rule is the authorization source. If the deduction is voluntary or contractual, there should be signed consent, a contract clause, or a documented policy approved by management. The payroll file should show who approved the deduction, why it is allowed, how much is being deducted, and whether the deduction is one-time or recurring. For deductions that affect future payrolls, the authorization should be reviewed whenever the employee’s status changes or the contract is amended. This is important because payroll deductions reduce net pay, and net pay disputes often arise when the employee cannot see the basis for the reduction. Proper authorization protects both the organization and the staff member by keeping the deduction traceable and reviewable.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep written authorization for every non-statutory deduction and retain the source document in the employee file.”
Key point:
- Deductions must be authorized and traceable.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- GAO Green Book
64. Q: What is the difference between employee deductions and employer costs?
A: Employee deductions are amounts taken from the employee’s gross pay, so they reduce the employee’s net pay. Employer costs are additional payroll expenses that the organization must bear on top of the employee’s gross wages and deductions, and they do not reduce net pay unless the law or contract specifically provides otherwise. This distinction matters because payroll reports can show both types side by side, but the accounting treatment is different. Employee deductions are usually liabilities or pass-through items that must be paid to another party, while employer costs are direct expenses of the organization. If the distinction is blurred, the organization may understate payroll expense or overstate employee withholding balances. Clean segregation also makes donor reporting and budgeting easier because the organization can see the true cost of employment rather than just the amount paid to staff.
Send to the team:
- “Please separate employee deductions from employer payroll expenses in the ledger and payroll register.”
Key point:
- Deductions affect the employee; employer costs affect the organization.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- IRS Topic No. 751
- IRS Publication 15-B
65. Q: What is employer social contribution?
A: Employer social contribution is the organization’s own payroll-related cost that arises because the employer has a legal obligation tied to employment. It may include employer social insurance, employer payroll tax, or similar statutory cost depending on the jurisdiction. It should be treated as an expense of the organization, not as a deduction from employee net pay. That means it belongs in payroll cost reporting and budget planning, and it should be accrued in the same period as the wage expense it relates to. Mixing employer contribution with employee deduction creates confusion because one is a cost of employing staff and the other is money withheld from staff and held for remittance. A clean payroll process should therefore show employer social contributions separately in both the payroll register and the general ledger.
Send to the team:
- “Please book employer social contributions as a separate payroll cost and do not net them against employee deductions.”
Key point:
- Employer social contributions are organizational expenses.
Official links:
- IRS Topic No. 751
- IRS Publication 15
66. Q: How should employer payroll taxes be budgeted?
A: Employer payroll taxes should be budgeted as part of the full employment cost, not as an afterthought added once salaries are already approved. The budget should start with headcount, salary grade, allowance assumptions, overtime assumptions, bonus assumptions, and expected hiring or turnover, then apply the relevant statutory rates. It is often useful to maintain a sensitivity view so finance can see the impact of vacancies, recruitments, and salary changes on the total payroll tax burden. Because payroll tax rates and labor patterns can change during the year, the budget should be reviewed during reforecast cycles rather than assumed fixed forever. This approach helps management see the true cost of staffing and prevents payroll from becoming a hidden overspend area. In an NGO, payroll tax budgeting also supports donor proposals, grant costing, and reserve planning because the organization knows the real employer cost of delivering programs.
Send to the team:
- “Please budget payroll taxes together with salaries, allowances, and headcount plans.”
Key point:
- Payroll tax budgets should follow the staffing plan and current statutory rate assumptions.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- GAO Green Book
67. Q: What is a payslip?
A: A payslip is the employee’s formal pay statement for a specific pay period. It explains how gross pay was calculated, what deductions were taken, what employer-related items may be shown, and what net pay was actually transferred to the employee. It is a transparency document, but it is also a control document because it allows the employee and payroll team to check that the amounts are correct. A good payslip should make the payroll calculation understandable without requiring the employee to ask the finance team for basic clarification every month. In practice, the payslip is one of the most visible proof points that payroll is being processed in a controlled and consistent way. When payslips are missing, late, or unclear, confidence in the whole payroll process tends to fall quickly.
Send to the team:
- “Please issue a clear payslip for every payroll cycle so staff can verify their pay and deductions.”
Key point:
- A payslip explains how net pay was calculated.
Official links:
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
- IRS Publication 15
68. Q: What information should appear on a payslip?
A: A payslip should show enough detail for the employee to trace the calculation of their earnings and deductions. At minimum, it should identify the employee, the pay period, the gross earnings, overtime or allowance items, statutory deductions, other deductions, and net pay. Where relevant, it is also helpful to show year-to-date totals, leave balances, or cumulative tax information so employees can understand how the current period fits within the year. The objective is not to overload the payslip with unnecessary detail, but to make the calculation verifiable and transparent. If the payslip is too brief, employees cannot validate it; if it is too confusing, staff may suspect errors even when the numbers are correct. A practical payroll design therefore balances clarity, detail, and readability.
Send to the team:
- “Please ensure payslips show every item that affects gross pay, deductions, and net pay.”
Key point:
- Payslips should let employees trace their pay calculation.
Official links:
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
- DOL FLSA
69. Q: Why should employees receive payslips?
A: Employees should receive payslips because payroll is more than a bank transfer; it is an explanation of how the transfer was calculated. A payslip gives the employee a chance to confirm gross pay, deductions, and net pay, and it allows issues to be raised quickly before the error becomes difficult to correct. It also reinforces transparency, which is especially important in organizations where pay structures may include allowances, project-based payments, or variable deductions. For finance, payslips create another layer of user review that can expose problems the payroll processor may have missed. In that sense, payslips are not just communication documents; they are part of the control system. When staff receive clear payslips regularly, payroll disputes usually become easier to manage and resolve.
Send to the team:
- “Please distribute payslips promptly after each payroll run so staff can review their pay without delay.”
Key point:
- Payslips make payroll review possible for employees.
Official links:
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
- GAO Green Book
70. Q: What is a payroll bank file?
A: A payroll bank file is the payment instruction file that tells the bank which employee accounts should receive salary payments and for what amounts. It is usually produced after payroll calculation and approval, so it should reflect the final validated payroll register rather than draft data. Because it directly releases cash, the file should be treated as a controlled financial file with limited access, change protection, and clear version control. Any unauthorized edit to the bank file can lead to duplicate payments, wrong payees, or salary fraud. For that reason, payroll bank files should be generated from approved data, checked before upload, and stored with evidence of who released them and when. This makes the file both a payment tool and a control record.
Send to the team:
- “Please treat the payroll bank file as a controlled payment file that can only be released after review and approval.”
Key point:
- The payroll bank file is the final payment instruction.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IRS Publication 15
71. Q: Why should payroll bank files be reviewed before release?
A: Payroll bank files should always be reviewed because the last step before payment is the point where the financial risk becomes real. A review catches wrong account numbers, incorrect amounts, duplicate entries, unauthorized additions, and changes made after payroll approval. The review should compare the final bank file to the approved payroll register and should confirm that the total amount, the payment date, and the recipient list all match what was authorized. A maker-checker control is especially important here because the person who prepares the file should not be the only person who can release it. When this control is done properly, the organization reduces both fraud risk and honest error. The review becomes part of the audit trail showing that cash was released only after a separate control check.
Send to the team:
- “Please verify the payroll file against the approved payroll register before release and keep evidence of the review.”
Key point:
- File review is a control against payment error and fraud.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Green Book 2025
72. Q: What is the risk of duplicate payroll payment?
A: Duplicate payroll payment is a serious control failure because it means the organization paid the same salary or portion of salary more than once. The immediate effect is overpayment and unnecessary cash outflow, but the longer-term effect can include employee recovery issues, payroll distortion, possible disputed deductions, and extra administration to fix the record. Duplicate payment can also create reputational problems if the employee is asked to return money or if the organization repeatedly shows weak controls around salary release. Often the root cause is weak review of the payroll register, poor master data, or failure to block duplicate bank entries. Once a duplicate payment occurs, finance must document the cause, recover the excess if possible, and adjust the ledger so the payroll expense and cash balances remain accurate. Preventive review is cheaper than recovery after the fact.
Send to the team:
- “Please check for duplicate records before each salary run and block repeated bank instructions.”
Key point:
- Duplicate pay creates avoidable loss and cleanup work.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
73. Q: What is the role of bank signatories in payroll control?
A: Bank signatories provide the final human authorization over the release of cash from the bank. In payroll, their role is to confirm that the payment file is valid, the amounts match the approved payroll, the payment is within authority, and the release is appropriate for the pay cycle. This role matters because payroll is one of the most sensitive cash transactions in any organization, and signatory control helps prevent unauthorized or mistaken payments. Signatories should not rely only on verbal confirmation; they should review documented evidence such as the payroll register, approval sheet, and bank file summary. Where feasible, the signatory should be independent from payroll preparation so the same person does not control both the data and the release. That separation strengthens accountability and makes fraud or error easier to detect.
Send to the team:
- “Please require signatory approval before payroll bank release and retain the approval evidence.”
Key point:
- Signatories help prevent unauthorized salary payments.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
74. Q: How should payroll payments be authorized?
A: Payroll payments should follow a documented approval chain that shows who prepared the payroll, who reviewed it, and who released the bank file. The authorization should confirm gross pay, deductions, employer costs where relevant, and net pay before any cash leaves the organization’s bank account. Ideally, the person who prepares the payroll should not be the person who releases it, and any manual override should be documented separately. The approval trail should include the payroll period, the total payment amount, the exception list, and the final release reference. This process gives management a reliable control point before money is transferred and creates a record that can be checked later by audit or by management. Payroll authorization is strongest when it is routine, documented, and tied to an agreed threshold and signatory matrix.
Send to the team:
- “Please use maker-checker approval for each payroll release and do not bypass the approval trail.”
Key point:
- Payroll authorization should happen before cash disbursement.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IRS Publication 15
75. Q: How should payroll advances be handled?
A: Payroll advances should be treated as controlled employee balances, not as informal extra salary. The advance should be approved in advance, recorded in a separate receivable or advance account, and tied to a clear recovery plan so payroll can deduct or settle it in a later period. The approval should show the reason for the advance, the employee name, the amount, the recovery schedule, and any exceptional treatment approved by management. Without this structure, advances can easily become unreconciled balances that are forgotten or incorrectly expensed. Finance should review the advance register each payroll cycle until the balance is fully settled, because an advance that is not monitored becomes a loss or a reporting error. Proper treatment keeps the payroll expense honest and prevents cash leakage through unmanaged staff balances.
Send to the team:
- “Please track every payroll advance until it is fully settled and do not leave any advance off the register.”
Key point:
- An advance is a tracked balance, not free extra pay.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IRS Publication 15
76. Q: What is a salary advance?
A: A salary advance is a payment made before the normal payroll date, usually against future earned salary or under a controlled staff loan arrangement. It is not the same as ordinary salary because the timing and recovery logic are different. The organization should approve the advance before payment, state the reason, and define how and when the amount will be recovered. If the amount is substantial or recurring, finance should record it as an employee balance that must be monitored separately from normal payroll expense. Salary advances can solve short-term staff needs, but without control they can also create confusion about unpaid balances, deductions, and final settlement. A good policy makes sure the organization helps staff without losing track of cash or salary deductions.
Send to the team:
- “Please treat salary advances as controlled employee balances with approval and recovery rules.”
Key point:
- Salary advances must be tracked and recovered.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
77. Q: How should salary advances be tracked?
A: Salary advances should be tracked in a live register that shows the employee’s name, ID, date of advance, amount, reason, approver, recovery method, amount recovered to date, and remaining balance. The register should be reviewed at every payroll cycle so recovery is not delayed or forgotten. If an employee leaves, finance should confirm the outstanding balance before final settlement and recovery from final pay where lawful and approved. The register should also identify advances that have aged beyond the expected recovery period so management can escalate them. This kind of tracking turns an advance from a vague promise into a visible balance that can be monitored and cleared. Without a register, advances quickly disappear into payroll noise and become a control weakness.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep a live register of all salary advances and review it every payroll cycle.”
Key point:
- Tracking prevents advances from being forgotten.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
78. Q: What is the risk of unpaid payroll advances?
A: Unpaid payroll advances can become unrecovered employee balances that distort the books and create avoidable losses. If the amount is left open too long, it may also be misclassified, forgotten, or wrongly offset against the wrong period’s payroll, which creates further reporting problems. In some cases the problem is only timing, but in many cases the issue points to weak follow-up and poor payroll monitoring. Unrecovered advances also make it harder to know what a real payroll expense is versus what is still owed back to the organization. The main control issue is that the balance should be actively reviewed and either recovered, approved for extension, or escalated for write-off only with proper authorization. Ignoring old advances can make a payroll process look smaller and cleaner than it really is.
Send to the team:
- “Please review open advances until they are cleared, recovered, or formally escalated.”
Key point:
- Unrecovered advances create loss and reporting risk.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
79. Q: How should terminated staff be handled in payroll?
A: Terminated staff should be removed from active payroll only after the termination is approved and communicated through the proper HR or management channel. Their final pay should be computed according to contract, policy, law, and any outstanding entitlements such as leave, bonuses, or unpaid allowances that are due. At the same time, finance should check for outstanding advances, unreturned assets, deductions, and any final recoveries that are allowed. The payroll master data should be closed so the person cannot appear in later payroll cycles, and the final settlement should be documented and reconciled. This helps prevent ghost payroll, duplicate pay, and unresolved balances after the employee has left. Termination handling is one of the most important controls in payroll because it combines HR control, cash control, and ledger accuracy in one process.
Send to the team:
- “Please stop payroll access immediately when termination is approved and complete final settlement before closure.”
Key point:
- Termination controls prevent ghost payroll and unrecovered balances.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IRS Publication 15
80. Q: What controls should apply to new hires in payroll?
A: New hires should enter payroll only after the organization has completed a controlled onboarding process. That process should include HR approval, a signed contract or appointment letter, identity verification, bank account verification, start-date confirmation, and correct setup of the salary grade and benefit package. Payroll should not be created from an informal message, a verbal instruction, or an unverified email. The control objective is to ensure the new person exists, is authorized, and is paid only at the approved rate from the approved start date. If controls are weak at the start, the payroll system can end up with wrong rates, duplicate records, or unauthorized staff entries that are difficult to fix later. A disciplined new-hire process protects the organization from the first payment onward.
Send to the team:
- “Please verify every new hire before entering them into payroll and keep the approval trail on file.”
Key point:
- New hire controls stop unauthorized or duplicate payroll entries.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
81. Q: What is retroactive salary adjustment?
A: A retroactive salary adjustment is a pay correction that applies back to an earlier date. It may arise from a promotion, a salary increase, a contractual revision, a grading correction, or a payroll error that is being corrected after the original pay run. Because the adjustment affects a prior period, the accounting team should document the reason, the effective date, the calculation basis, and the period or periods affected. Retroactive changes are especially sensitive in NGO payroll because they may affect donor charging, project costing, and period reporting. If the organization does not capture the effective date clearly, the payroll correction may be booked in the wrong month and distort both expense and liability balances. A controlled retroactive adjustment process keeps the correction accurate and auditable.
Send to the team:
- “Please document the reason and effective date for every retroactive pay change before payment is processed.”
Key point:
- Retroactive changes need clear support and approval.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- GAO Green Book
82. Q: How should retroactive pay changes be approved?
A: Retroactive pay changes should be approved through a clear management or HR review before payroll is updated. The approval should confirm that the change is legitimate, that the effective date is correct, and that the adjustment matches the contract, policy, or official amendment that created the entitlement. Finance should then recalculate the impacted payroll periods and retain the evidence so the change can be traced from approval to posting. This protects the organization from informal backdated changes that can be difficult to explain later. Retroactive changes should never be processed casually because they often affect prior reporting periods, accrued liabilities, and donor-charged costs. A strong approval process makes the correction transparent and prevents confusion when reconciliations are performed later.
Send to the team:
- “Please require written approval before any retroactive payroll change is paid or posted.”
Key point:
- Retroactive pay changes should never be informal.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IRS Publication 15
83. Q: What is a payroll journal entry?
A: A payroll journal entry is the accounting entry that converts payroll processing into ledger form. It records gross wages, employee deductions, employer payroll costs, payroll liabilities, and the cash or bank impact where appropriate. In other words, the payroll journal is the bridge between the payroll register and the general ledger, and it should follow the approved payroll calculation exactly unless there is a documented adjustment. If the payroll journal is prepared incorrectly, the ledger may show the wrong expense, the wrong payable, or the wrong liability balance. A good payroll journal is supported by a register, an approval sheet, and a reconciliation showing how the figures were derived. That makes month-end close faster and gives auditors a clear starting point for testing payroll completeness and accuracy.
Send to the team:
- “Please post payroll journals from the approved payroll register and retain the support package.”
Key point:
- The payroll journal links payroll processing to accounting.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- GAO Green Book
84. Q: Why should payroll journals be reviewed?
A: Payroll journals should be reviewed because payroll is one of the easiest areas for small mistakes to repeat across many staff members. Review catches wrong account codes, missing employer costs, misallocated project charges, unusual deductions, and items booked in the wrong period. It also gives the finance team a chance to compare the journal to the register, the payroll summary, and the bank payment file before the numbers are locked in. A review that only checks the total amount is not enough; the account structure and period logic matter too. Once the journal is posted, correcting it may require reversals and reclassifications, so review before posting is always cheaper than cleanup later. The review step therefore protects both the integrity of the ledger and the usefulness of the management report.
Send to the team:
- “Please review payroll journals before they are finalized and posted.”
Key point:
- Review reduces posting errors and misstated payroll costs.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
85. Q: How should payroll be allocated to projects or cost centers?
A: Payroll should be allocated using a basis that is documented, defensible, and consistent. Common allocation drivers include timesheets, staff time schedules, approved labor distribution percentages, or role-based allocation rules that reflect how the employee actually works. The important control point is that the chosen method should match the work reality as closely as possible and should not be changed casually from month to month. Finance should retain the source evidence behind the allocation so a donor, auditor, or manager can see why the cost was charged to a specific project or cost center. If the payroll split is not supportable, the reported project cost becomes misleading and may cause issues in grant reporting or recovery. Good allocation links payroll processing to program activity in a way that is fair and reviewable.
Send to the team:
- “Please allocate payroll only using a documented and reviewed basis that reflects actual work performed.”
Key point:
- Payroll allocation needs a defensible driver.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
86. Q: What is the relationship between payroll and cost allocation?
A: Payroll is the raw employment cost, while cost allocation decides where that cost should be charged in the accounting records. Payroll processing determines what the employee earned and what the organization owes, but allocation determines whether that cost is charged to a program, a project, overhead, administration, or some other reporting bucket. The two processes are related but not identical, and confusing them often causes errors in donor reports. A payroll register may be correct, but if the allocation schedule is wrong, the management reports can still be misleading. That is why finance teams should separate “pay calculation” from “cost distribution” in both their process design and their documentation. When the allocation basis is clear, payroll expense becomes more useful for budgeting, donor reporting, and management analysis.
Send to the team:
- “Please separate payroll processing from payroll allocation logic so the ledger shows both the cost and the correct destination.”
Key point:
- Payroll creates the cost; allocation assigns it.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
87. Q: Why is staff cost allocation important for grants?
A: Staff cost allocation is critical for grants because salaries and related payroll costs often make up a large share of donor-funded expenditure. If the allocation is wrong, the organization may charge the wrong project, overstate a budget line, or report unsupported costs to a donor. Many grant agreements expect labor charges to be supported by timesheets, agreed allocation rules, or other evidence showing how staff time was used. That means payroll allocation is not only an internal accounting matter; it is also a grant compliance issue. When allocation is weak, the organization risks audit findings, claw-backs, and problems with future funding because donors want confidence that staff time was charged correctly. A disciplined allocation process therefore protects both reporting accuracy and donor trust.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep grant payroll allocations supported by timesheets or approved allocation rules.”
Key point:
- Grant payroll must be supportable and traceable.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
88. Q: What is a staff time allocation schedule?
A: A staff time allocation schedule shows how an employee’s working time is split across projects, cost centers, or activities over a defined period. It is a planning and reporting tool that helps finance decide how much payroll cost to charge to each area of work. The schedule usually reflects approved percentages or actual time usage, depending on the organization’s policy. It is particularly useful when staff work across multiple grants or when one person supports both program and support functions. A good schedule should be clear enough to explain the logic behind the payroll split and stable enough to support recurring monthly posting. Without it, payroll allocation becomes guesswork rather than controlled accounting.
Send to the team:
- “Please maintain time allocation schedules for staff whose costs are split across more than one activity or project.”
Key point:
- Time allocation supports payroll costing.
Official links:
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
- GAO Green Book
89. Q: How should staff time be documented for allocation purposes?
A: Staff time should be documented in a way that shows the basis for the payroll split and who reviewed it. Depending on the organization’s policy, this may include timesheets, activity logs, supervisor review, signed allocation forms, or approved labor distribution plans. The record should make it possible to trace a percentage of payroll back to actual work performed or to a documented allocation rule. If the organization uses an estimated allocation rather than exact time records, that estimate still needs management approval and periodic review. The value of the documentation is that it turns a payroll allocation from an assumption into a supportable record. That matters for audits, grant reporting, and internal cost analysis.
Send to the team:
- “Please support all staff cost splits with dated time records or approved allocation evidence.”
Key point:
- Documentation is the evidence for cost allocation.
Official links:
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
- GAO Green Book
90. Q: What is the risk of misallocating employee costs?
A: Misallocating employee costs can have financial, compliance, and governance consequences. It can distort project results, understate or overstate indirect costs, and create reporting errors in donor submissions or management accounts. In a grant-funded environment, wrong labor allocation can also mean the organization charges a project for work that was not actually done for that project, which may lead to questioned costs or audit findings. The risk is not limited to overcharging; undercharging can also hide the true cost of a project and make future budgeting inaccurate. Often the cause is weak coding, weak review, or no clear allocation basis at the start of the process. Correcting the problem after month-end is possible, but it is always more expensive than getting the allocation right from the beginning.
Send to the team:
- “Please check payroll splits before posting them to projects and escalate any unusual allocation immediately.”
Key point:
- Misallocation can affect compliance, donor trust, and reporting accuracy.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
91. Q: How should payroll confidentiality be maintained?
A: Payroll confidentiality should be treated as a normal control requirement, not as an optional courtesy. Access to payroll data should be limited to staff who genuinely need it, and roles should be separated so that not everyone can see salaries, deductions, bank details, or personal employee records. Files should be stored securely, transfers should be protected, and printed reports should be controlled or destroyed when no longer needed. Confidentiality also means employees should not see one another’s pay information unless there is a valid business reason. If payroll data is exposed casually, it can create privacy problems, employee distrust, and misuse of personal financial information. A strong confidentiality practice supports both ethical conduct and control integrity.
Send to the team:
- “Please restrict payroll data to authorized staff only and store it in secure, need-to-know files.”
Key point:
- Payroll data should be protected like sensitive financial information.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
92. Q: What is a payroll exception log?
A: A payroll exception log is a control register for unusual payroll events. It records manual overrides, off-cycle payments, corrections, disputed deductions, retroactive changes, unusual allowances, and any item that does not follow the ordinary monthly payroll process. The purpose of the log is to make exceptions visible so they can be reviewed instead of hidden inside a normal payroll run. Each entry should show what happened, why it happened, who approved it, what the financial impact was, and whether a correction or recovery is required. If the log is used properly, management can identify repeat patterns, see where training or system changes are needed, and monitor the real risk areas in payroll. An exception log is therefore both a record and a management tool.
Send to the team:
- “Please log every payroll exception with a reason, approver, and impact so unusual items can be reviewed later.”
Key point:
- Exception logs make unusual payroll items visible.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
93. Q: What should be reviewed in a payroll exception log?
A: The payroll exception log should be reviewed not only for the event itself, but for the cause behind it. Review should show the reason for the exception, the approval trail, the amount affected, the account or project charged, the recovery or correction plan, and whether similar items keep happening. Management should ask whether the exception was planned, unavoidable, or a sign of a weakness in the process. If the same type of exception appears repeatedly, the problem is likely systemic and needs a control change rather than another one-off fix. The review should also confirm that the exception was reflected correctly in the payroll register and ledger. This turns exception handling from a reactive task into a learning tool for payroll improvement.
Send to the team:
- “Please review payroll exceptions for root cause, recurrence, and control improvement, not just the immediate correction.”
Key point:
- Exception review should lead to control improvement.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
94. Q: What is payroll reconciliation with HR records?
A: Payroll reconciliation with HR records means checking that the payroll register agrees with the organization’s human resources master data. That includes contracts, hiring dates, termination dates, grades, salary changes, approved allowances, and leave or absence records where they affect pay. The goal is to confirm that payroll is paying only authorized staff, at the authorized rate, for the authorized period. When payroll and HR do not agree, the problem could be a wrong start date, a missed termination, an outdated salary grade, or a correction that was never fed into payroll. Reconciliation between the two systems is therefore one of the most powerful defenses against ghost employees, overpayments, and payroll setup errors. It should be done every cycle, not only when a problem appears.
Send to the team:
- “Please reconcile payroll to HR master data every cycle and resolve any mismatch before the payroll file is released.”
Key point:
- HR-to-payroll matching helps prevent ghost employees and wrong rates.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
95. Q: Why should payroll agree to employee contracts?
A: Payroll should agree to employee contracts because the contract is the formal source of authority for pay, allowances, deductions, and benefits. If the payroll setup does not match the contract, the organization may underpay, overpay, or incorrectly withhold amounts that were never properly agreed. This mismatch can also create employee disputes and make payroll corrections more complicated later. A contract review before payroll activation gives finance a clear basis for salary, benefits, effective dates, and any special conditions tied to the role. For control purposes, the payroll record should be able to point back to the contract or approved amendment that created the pay amount. This alignment is essential for both legal certainty and accounting accuracy.
Send to the team:
- “Please compare payroll setup to the signed contract before activation and whenever the contract changes.”
Key point:
- The contract is the source of payroll authority.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IRS Publication 15
96. Q: How should annual salary reviews be managed?
A: Annual salary reviews should follow a formal cycle so pay changes are deliberate rather than reactive. The organization should check affordability against the budget, consider performance or market adjustments where relevant, and obtain the required approval before payroll is updated. The effective date should be documented clearly so the salary change is recorded in the correct period and any retroactive effect can be calculated separately if needed. Salary review decisions should also be compared against the overall pay structure so similar roles are treated consistently and exceptions are visible. Once approved, the updated salary should be entered into payroll master data and reviewed before the next payment run. This process keeps compensation fair, controlled, and budget-aware.
Send to the team:
- “Please approve annual salary changes before they are entered into payroll and keep the budget check on file.”
Key point:
- Salary reviews need a controlled approval cycle.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IRS Publication 15
97. Q: What controls are needed for bonuses and incentives?
A: Bonuses and incentives need stronger control than ordinary payroll items because they are often variable, judgment-based, and budget-sensitive. The policy should define eligibility, approval thresholds, calculation rules, payment timing, and any performance conditions that must be met. Finance should check that the payment is within budget and that the tax treatment has been reviewed before release. The bonus should be visible in payroll records so the organization can explain why the payment was made and who approved it. If bonuses are not controlled, they can become hidden salary increases or inconsistent rewards that weaken fairness and budget discipline. Good bonus control makes sure the payment is earned, approved, and reported correctly.
Send to the team:
- “Please require approval, budget check, and tax review before any bonus or incentive is paid.”
Key point:
- Bonuses need policy, budget, and tax control.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15-B
- IRS Publication 15
- GAO Green Book
98. Q: What are common payroll errors in NGOs?
A: Common payroll errors include wrong pay rates, missing deductions, unapproved hires, duplicate payments, incorrect tax withholding, bad project allocation, and late remittance. In many cases, these errors are not caused by a single bad transaction but by weak master data, weak review controls, or unclear responsibility between HR and finance. NGOs are especially exposed when staff work across multiple projects, when allowances change often, or when payroll is handled manually without a strong checklist. A useful way to reduce these errors is to review the master data first, then the calculation, then the deductions, then the allocation, and finally the bank file. That sequence makes the review easier and helps the team find the root cause instead of only correcting the symptom. A disciplined process lowers the chance of repeated mistakes and keeps payroll reliable.
Send to the team:
- “Please review master data, deductions, allocations, and tax treatment before payroll release.”
Key point:
- Most payroll errors come from weak setup or weak review.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- GAO Green Book
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
99. Q: How can payroll controls strengthen nonprofit accountability?
A: Payroll controls strengthen nonprofit accountability by showing exactly how staff pay was calculated, approved, distributed, and posted. When the organization keeps a proper audit trail, it can demonstrate to donors, auditors, and management that salaries, deductions, employer taxes, and allocations were handled correctly. Controls also help the organization show that staff were paid in line with approved contracts and that payroll costs were assigned to the right programs or support functions. Without this structure, payroll becomes a black box where cash leaves the bank but the underlying logic is hard to explain. Strong controls therefore turn payroll into a traceable and reviewable process rather than a monthly administrative routine. That is a major improvement for transparency, governance, and budget control.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep a full audit trail for every payroll run from source document to ledger posting.”
Key point:
- Payroll controls turn payroll into an auditable process.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IRS Publication 15
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
100. Q: What does strong payroll, tax, and employee cost management look like in practice?
A: Strong payroll, tax, and employee cost management is a controlled cycle, not just a payment run. It starts with accurate master data, approved hires, approved salary changes, and documented benefits and deductions. It continues with correct tax withholding, proper liability accrual, clean project allocation, secure bank file release, and timely tax filing. It ends with reconciliations, exception review, and storage of the support package so the whole process can be audited later. In practice, strong payroll management means the payroll register, bank file, tax filings, and ledger all tell the same story, and any differences are explained and resolved promptly. When that happens consistently, payroll becomes reliable, budgetable, and trustworthy for both management and donors.
Send to the team:
- “Please operate payroll as a controlled process with approvals, checks, reconciliations, and exception review at every stage.”
Key point:
- Strong payroll management is accurate, documented, and reviewable.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 15
- GAO Green Book
- DOL Fact Sheet #21
Rows: 100
Table 10
Q&A Foreign Currency and Inter-Entity Accounting
1. Q: What is foreign currency accounting in NGO finance?
A: Foreign currency accounting is the process of identifying, recording, remeasuring, translating, and reporting transactions and balances that are denominated in a currency other than the NGO’s functional currency. In practical terms, the finance team cannot rely only on the amount shown on a donor transfer advice, supplier invoice, bank slip, or payment confirmation. It must also convert that amount into the currency used for the organization’s books and reports, because that is the currency in which the financial statements are measured and compared.

This matters in NGOs because the same transaction can move through several value layers. A donor may commit in USD, the field office may spend in local currency, and headquarters may report in another presentation currency. Exchange rates can change between approval, receipt, payment, and reporting, so the accounting record must preserve both the source-currency amount and the functional-currency effect. Good foreign currency accounting therefore supports stewardship, budget control, donor reporting, and liquidity planning at the same time.

It also helps management see the difference between operational performance and currency movement. Without that separation, a program may appear to overspend or underspend when the real issue is simply that the exchange rate moved after the budget was approved. The discipline is not only technical accounting; it is also operational clarity.
Send to the team:
- “Please record all non-functional currency items using the correct FX treatment and keep the source currency visible.”
Key point:
- Foreign currency items must be tracked in the functional currency.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
2. Q: Why is foreign currency important for nonprofits working across countries?
A: It matters because cross-border NGOs often receive funding in one currency, incur costs in another, and report results in a third. That creates currency exposure across the entire life of a transaction: grant signing, cash receipt, budget release, procurement, payroll, field spending, revaluation, and final reporting. A program may look fully funded in donor currency but still become financially tight in local currency once exchange movement, bank charges, timing delays, and settlement differences are included.

For management, the practical risk is that reported balances may look stronger than the real spending power available on the ground. A project with stable donor commitments can still experience pressure if local currency weakens and the same dollars or euros buy less program input than expected. That is why FX should appear not only in the ledger, but also in the budget, the cash forecast, the grant tracker, and the month-end commentary. When this is done properly, the organization can explain what happened and avoid confusing currency movement with poor program delivery.
Send to the team:
- “Please monitor all cross-border balances and spending in both currencies and explain FX effects in the report.”
Key point:
- Multi-country work creates FX exposure.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
3. Q: What is the difference between foreign currency and local currency?
A: Local currency is the currency the NGO uses as its functional currency for accounting, measurement, and reporting. Foreign currency is any other currency used in a transaction, held in a bank account, or owed by or to the organization. The distinction matters because accounting starts from the functional currency, not from the currency shown on the vendor bill or donor transfer.

In an NGO with multiple country offices, the same currency can be local in one entity and foreign in another. That means the team should never assume that one transaction has the same treatment everywhere. Once the functional currency is known, the finance team can determine what must be converted at initial recognition and what must later be remeasured at the reporting date. That single decision drives the rest of the FX process, including settlement accounting, balance-sheet revaluation, and reporting treatment.
Send to the team:
- “Please identify the functional currency before coding any foreign currency item.”
Key point:
- Functional currency drives accounting treatment.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
4. Q: What is the difference between a foreign currency transaction and a foreign currency balance?
A: A foreign currency transaction is the original event, such as receiving a donor payment, buying supplies, paying a supplier, or drawing down a loan in another currency. A foreign currency balance is the amount still outstanding after that event, such as an unpaid invoice, a receivable, a loan, or cash held in a foreign-currency bank account. The distinction matters because the original event is recorded once, while the open balance may need to be remeasured repeatedly until it is closed.

In practice, the transaction creates the exposure, but the balance is what remains exposed. That is why the accounting team should first understand whether it is dealing with the historical event or the continuing amount on the books. If the open balance is not tracked separately, month-end revaluation can be missed, and the realized exchange difference at settlement may be misstated. A disciplined process keeps the original transaction, the open balance, the rate used, and the closing remeasurement all visible in one audit trail.
Send to the team:
- “Please separate the original transaction from the open balance before posting FX adjustments.”
Key point:
- Transactions create balances that may need later remeasurement.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
5. Q: What is the difference between remeasurement and translation?
A: Remeasurement is the process of converting foreign currency items into the functional currency so they can be recorded properly in the ledger. Translation is the process of presenting financial statements from the functional currency into another presentation currency, usually for group reporting, donor consolidation, or head-office reporting. The first is item-level measurement; the second is statement-level presentation.

This difference matters because the accounting logic and the reporting purpose are not the same. Remeasurement asks what amount should sit in the books today, while translation asks how whole financial statements should be shown in another currency. If the team confuses the two, the wrong rate may be applied at the wrong level, and the resulting reports can become hard to reconcile. A sound process therefore identifies whether the task is measuring an item or presenting a full set of statements before any FX entry is posted.
Send to the team:
- “Please use remeasurement for items and translation for financial statements, and do not mix the two.”
Key point:
- Remeasurement is for items; translation is for statements.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
6. Q: What is the difference between settlement and revaluation?
A: Settlement is the actual payment or receipt that closes the foreign currency item. Revaluation is the period-end update of an open monetary item to the closing rate before that settlement happens. If an item is still unpaid or uncollected at month end, it should usually be revalued first, then settled later at the rate applicable on the settlement date.

The practical control point is that revaluation and settlement are two different accounting moments. Revaluation adjusts the carrying value while the item is still open. Settlement finalizes the item and produces the realized FX difference, if any. If the team posts the final cash movement without first updating the open balance, the final exchange difference can be distorted. A clean close sequence keeps those steps separate and makes the FX movement traceable from opening balance to final closure.
Send to the team:
- “Please revalue open monetary items before they are settled and record the settlement difference separately.”
Key point:
- Revaluation happens before settlement if the item is still open.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
7. Q: Why must NGOs track exchange rates carefully?
A: Because exchange rates determine the functional-currency value of foreign currency transactions and balances, and even a small movement can create a noticeable gain or loss. The issue is not only technical accounting accuracy. It also affects program reality, because the local-currency cost of goods, services, payroll, and field operations can change quickly.

A grant that looked adequate on the day it was approved may fund less activity later if the exchange rate moves unfavorably. The finance team therefore needs a clear rate source, a clear rate date, and a documented rule for which rate applies to which type of transaction. That makes the month-end file defensible and helps leadership understand why the numbers moved. It also prevents the same balance from being revalued inconsistently across offices or reporting periods.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep a clear source for all exchange rates used and attach it to the month-end file.”
Key point:
- Exchange rates directly affect reported values.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
8. Q: What exchange rate should be used when recording a foreign currency transaction?
A: The usual starting point is the spot exchange rate on the date the transaction first qualifies for recognition. In some cases, an average rate can be used as a practical approximation if it reasonably reflects the actual rates in the period and if rates are not highly volatile. The important discipline is consistency, support, and a clear rule for the type of transaction being recorded.

The finance team should be able to show why a rate was selected, where it came from, and whether it was the right rate for that transaction type. If the organization has donor-funded transactions across many locations, this becomes even more important because different offices may access different rate sources. A rate policy, a documented source list, and a month-end review of the rate used will reduce disputes later when the transaction is audited or compared against donor reporting.
Send to the team:
- “Please use the transaction-date spot rate or a valid approximation and document the basis for it.”
Key point:
- Initial recognition uses the transaction-date rate.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
9. Q: What exchange rate should be used at month end for foreign currency balances?
A: Monetary foreign currency balances should be translated using the closing rate at the reporting date. That means the open balance is not left at the original transaction-date amount if the currency has moved since the item was first recorded. The month-end rate brings the balance back to a current measurement basis so the financial statements reflect the latest value.

This is one of the main reasons month-end close is so important in an organization with foreign currency exposure. If remeasurement is delayed or skipped, the balance sheet can understate or overstate the receivable or obligation, and the exchange difference will be pushed into the wrong period. A strong process therefore reviews all open foreign currency monetary items at close, applies the closing rate, and then confirms that any difference is posted to the correct FX line. That gives management a more honest picture of the entity’s financial position.
Send to the team:
- “Please revalue open monetary balances at the month-end closing rate and check the resulting FX difference.”
Key point:
- Month-end monetary balances use the closing rate.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
10. Q: What causes exchange gains and losses?
A: Exchange gains and losses arise because the exchange rate changes between the date the item was first recorded, the date it is remeasured, and the date it is finally settled. If the foreign currency strengthens or weakens against the functional currency, the carrying amount of the asset or liability changes even though the underlying cash flow may not have changed. The gain or loss therefore reflects currency movement rather than operational performance.

That distinction is very important in NGO reporting. A program can miss budget, meet budget, or overperform, and the FX result can still move the final surplus or deficit independently. This is why FX differences should be presented separately from program income and program spending. It gives management, donors, and boards a clearer view of what was driven by implementation and what was driven by currency movement. The more foreign currency exposure an NGO has, the more important this separation becomes for analysis and decision-making.
Send to the team:
- “Please review all open foreign currency balances for exchange differences and classify them correctly.”
Key point:
- Rate movement creates exchange differences.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
11. Q: How should exchange differences be recorded?
A: Exchange differences on monetary items are generally recognized in profit or loss in the period in which they arise, unless a specific exception applies. That means the accounting team should not bury them inside ordinary expenses or treat them as part of the original transaction cost. They need to be visible so management can separate business performance from currency movement.

In an NGO, that visibility matters because donor reports, budget-to-actual reports, and management commentary often need to explain why the reported result changed. If FX is hidden inside ordinary operating lines, the report can become misleading and the root cause of a variance can be lost. The clean approach is to use dedicated FX gain and loss accounts, reconcile them to the revaluation schedule, and keep supporting evidence for the rate, balance, and posting date. That gives both auditors and internal reviewers a traceable path from the source item to the final ledger result.
Send to the team:
- “Please post exchange differences to the correct gain or loss account and keep them separate from operating lines.”
Key point:
- Most exchange differences go through profit or loss.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
12. Q: Why do exchange differences matter in NGO reporting?
A: They matter because they can alter the reported surplus or deficit without any real change in the underlying activity. They also affect how budgets are compared to actuals, especially when the budget is prepared in one currency and expenditures are made in another. If the FX impact is not clearly shown, program managers may think they overspent when the real issue was currency movement.

Separating exchange differences from operating results makes reports more honest and easier to explain to leadership and donors. It also helps the organization decide whether a variance came from implementation, timing, pricing, or currency exposure. For a multi-country NGO, this is not a minor accounting detail; it is part of the management story. Clear FX reporting supports better budgeting, better forecasting, and better follow-up when results do not match the original plan.
Send to the team:
- “Please show exchange differences separately in the reporting pack and explain whether they are realized or unrealized.”
Key point:
- FX differences can change reported performance.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
13. Q: What is a foreign currency bank account?
A: It is a bank account maintained in a currency different from the NGO’s functional currency. The amount in the bank statement is therefore not yet the amount that will appear in the ledger or reporting pack in functional currency terms. Because the account is exposed to exchange movement, its value in the books can change even if no cash is deposited or withdrawn.

That is why foreign currency bank accounts need both bank reconciliation and FX retranslation at period end. A clean close process should identify the balance in account currency, the rate used for translation, and the resulting functional-currency balance. This is especially important where grants are held in a donor currency while local spending is done in another currency. The bank account is not just a place to keep cash; it is also a live FX exposure that needs routine review.
Send to the team:
- “Please track the bank balance in both account currency and functional currency.”
Key point:
- Foreign currency bank accounts still need FX treatment.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
14. Q: How should a foreign currency bank account be reconciled?
A: First reconcile the bank statement to the ledger in the account currency so all receipts, payments, charges, and transfers are matched. Then translate the reconciled ending balance into the functional currency using the closing rate and compare that value to the ledger carrying amount. Any difference created by exchange movement should be recorded clearly as an FX difference, not hidden inside unexplained reconciling items.

This gives the organization one clean reconciliation that covers both cash movement and currency movement. It also makes it easier to see whether the difference arose from timing, bank charges, transfer lag, or genuine FX change. A solid monthly close will also check that foreign bank balances agree across the cashbook, bank reconciliation, treasury schedule, and trial balance. That is the point where cash control, FX control, and reporting control meet in one document.
Send to the team:
- “Please reconcile the foreign currency bank account in both currencies and attach the rate used at month end.”
Key point:
- Reconciliation must cover both the bank and the FX translation.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
15. Q: What is the risk of holding cash in multiple currencies?
A: The risk is that the functional value of cash can change before the organization uses it, so the purchasing power of the balance may fall or rise without any movement in the bank. This can create liquidity pressure even when the bank account still looks healthy in nominal terms. A team may believe it has enough funds to complete a program, only to find that exchange movement has reduced the local value available for spending.

Multi-currency cash therefore needs tighter planning, not just passive reporting. The liquidity dashboard should show balances in both foreign currency and functional currency so the finance team can see real spend capacity. Where the NGO has several operating currencies, management should also know which balances are earmarked for near-term commitments and which are still exposed to rate movement. That makes treasury a planning function, not only an administrative one.
Send to the team:
- “Please monitor multi-currency cash positions daily or weekly and show the functional-currency equivalent.”
Key point:
- Multi-currency cash creates valuation risk.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
16. Q: How should an NGO monitor currency risk?
A: It should track all foreign currency cash, receivables, payables, advances, loans, and grant balances, then compare those exposures against expected inflows and outflows by currency. The purpose is not merely to list balances, but to understand the direction and timing of the risk. If the NGO expects to pay a supplier in three months but holds the money in a different currency today, the exposure needs to be visible well before settlement.

Regular monitoring also gives management time to decide whether to accelerate spending, convert cash, renegotiate payment timing, or update forecasts. A practical FX exposure report should show currency, balance type, due date, and whether the item is an asset or liability. That report should sit alongside the cash forecast and grant utilization tracker so the organization can see both accounting exposure and operational impact. The goal is not to eliminate all FX risk, but to understand it early enough to act.
Send to the team:
- “Please maintain a monthly FX exposure report by currency, due date, and balance type.”
Key point:
- Currency risk should be measured by exposure.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
17. Q: What is currency exposure?
A: Currency exposure is the amount of value that could change because the organization must receive or pay money in a foreign currency. It exists whenever the NGO has an open foreign currency asset or liability and is not yet fully settled. Exposure is what turns ordinary transactions into financial risk, because the final local-currency value is still uncertain until the item is closed.

The larger the exposure and the longer it remains open, the more likely the FX effect will matter to the budget and reported results. Exposure is therefore a management concept as much as an accounting one. It tells leadership where value may move before settlement and where control attention should be concentrated. A clean exposure schedule can also help treasury prioritize which balances should be paid, converted, or monitored more closely.
Send to the team:
- “Please measure exposure by currency, balance type, and due date.”
Key point:
- Exposure is the amount affected by FX movement.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
18. Q: Why is currency exposure important for budgeting?
A: It matters because the local-currency cost of activities can change before the NGO spends the money, which can create overspending or under-spending even when the original donor budget looked sufficient. This is especially true for imports, travel, payroll support, and cross-border procurement. A budget without FX sensitivity can give a false sense of certainty.

A better budget includes a base rate and one or more stress scenarios so managers can see what happens if the currency moves against them. For NGO planning, FX sensitivity should be treated as part of scenario design, not as an unusual exception. That makes budget review more realistic and helps leadership understand whether a shortfall is caused by implementation or by rate movement. It also improves donor communication when a reforecast is needed later in the year.
Send to the team:
- “Please budget with an FX sensitivity range, not one fixed rate only.”
Key point:
- Budgeting should allow for rate movement.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
19. Q: What is a foreign currency payable?
A: It is an obligation to pay a fixed or determinable amount in a foreign currency, such as an unpaid supplier invoice, a loan installment, or a service fee owed to a cross-border vendor. Because it is a monetary liability, its functional-currency value can move before payment is made. That means the balance on the books may change from one reporting date to the next even though the original invoice amount remains the same.

The finance team needs to know both the original foreign amount and the current functional amount so the obligation is not understated or overstated. Foreign currency payables should be aged and remeasured together so the team can see both settlement timing and FX movement. In an NGO environment, this is especially important for procurement and field operations, where delayed payment can change the local value of a budget line quickly. The payable schedule should therefore show amount, currency, vendor, due date, and revaluation status.
Send to the team:
- “Please list all foreign currency payables separately from local currency payables.”
Key point:
- Payables in other currencies must be remeasured.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
20. Q: What is a foreign currency receivable?
A: It is an amount the NGO expects to receive in a foreign currency, such as a donor receivable, a grant reimbursement, a customer invoice, or a refund due from a supplier. Because it is an asset rather than a liability, exchange movement can increase or decrease the amount of functional currency that will ultimately be collected. This makes the receivable more than a simple admin balance; it is also a value that can shift before settlement.

The organization should therefore monitor aging, due date, and exchange movement together instead of treating them as separate topics. A receivable that looks current in foreign currency terms may still be exposed to a meaningful FX change in functional currency terms. The practical control is to keep the open-item listing current, remeasure it at close, and compare it with collection expectations. That way the finance team can distinguish a slow-paying counterparty from a genuine exchange movement effect.
Send to the team:
- “Please track foreign currency receivables by currency, aging, and expected collection date.”
Key point:
- Receivables in other currencies also need FX treatment.
Official links:
- IFRS IAS 21 full standard
- IFRS IAS 21 overview
- IFRS IAS 21 supporting material
21. Q: How should foreign currency payables be measured?
A: They should be measured initially at the transaction-date spot rate and then remeasured at each reporting date using the closing rate until the obligation is settled. The same invoice can therefore have more than one functional-currency value during its life on the books. That is normal and expected for a monetary liability. The important control is that the remeasurement is performed consistently and the resulting exchange difference is recorded in the right period.

If the liability is long outstanding, the FX impact can become material enough to distort both the balance sheet and the operating result if it is not kept up to date. The finance team should therefore keep a monthly revaluation schedule, tie it to the subledger, and make sure every open foreign-currency liability is on the close checklist.
